(RECOMMENDED after you confirm correlation) 11) If connector/NDES or Defender sensor root cause confirmed (DOCUMENTED fix) - DOCUMENTED remediation used previously: adjust firewall rules to allow the connector, reinstall connector without proxy if required, temporary MF A exclusion for the service account + elevate role if required, validate connector shows "green" in Azure, then revert the temporary exception and monitor. The Intune/NDES and Defender incidents show these steps were performed and documented [Data: Sources (35); Reports (14)]. (RECOMMENDED after you confirm correlation) C - What the records do NOT contain / why this cannot be fully resolved from the archive alone - The indexed incidents show the exact diagnostic/artifact types you must collect (ADFS client registration exports, Azure AD sign‑in logs, Microsoft Graph group exports, NSG/firewall config‑audits, WAF/load‑balancer access logs, SMTP relay logs, connector logs, DC event logs). The archive does not contain the live tenant logs, the firewall audit exports, the WAF runtime config snapshots, or the Azure AD Connect run logs for your current incident window - you must collect these from your environment to establish causation and then apply the documented remediations above [DOCUMENTED requirement; the corpus does not include current logs] [Data: Sources (15); Sources (13); Sources (35); Sources (14); Sources (32)]. D - Classification recommendation (explicit, with record evidence) - Based on the pattern of simultaneous, cross‑domain failures (non‑browser auth, SharePoint guest access, SMTP desktop send, NDES/connector + sensor health), the indexed records show such incidents previously required escalation to specialist engineering teams (Identity/ADFS, BU Network & Security, Exchange/Hybrid, Endpoint/Intune). Prior cases of the same pattern (e.g., TI‑1594408, TI‑1385257, TI‑1179834, TI‑722423) were handled as second_level_support and required cross‑team coordination [DOCUMENTED pattern]. Therefore this ticket should be classified as second_level_support and escalated immediately to Identity + Network + Mail + Endpoint engineering with the evidence bundle described above [Data: Sources (15); Sources (11); Sources (14); Sources (35)]. E - Minimal evidence bundle to attach to the escalation (recommended; these are the exact items that prior incidents used) (Please produce these from your tenant / devices and attach to the escalation ticket.) 1. ADFS client registration export (PROD & TEST) for the DevOps integration client - include EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders flag values [Supported by prior fix] [Data: Sources (11)]. 2. Azure AD sign‑in log slices (UTC) for representative service accounts and affected users (time window: first alert −30m -> remediation +2h) with CA evaluation results and token claim payloads [Used in prior investigations] [Data: Sources (31); Reports (30)]. 3. Microsoft Graph group membership export for the SharePoint folder groups (objectId, displayName, member objectIds, member source) and Azure AD Connect sync logs for the same window [Used to find cloud vs on‑prem collisions] [Data: Sources (13)]. 4. Exchange Message Trace and on‑prem Exchange event log exports (EventID 535 excerpts) for affected mailboxes and the Outbox send attempts [Used in prior SMTP AUTH cases] [Data: Sources (32)]. 5. Intune NDES connector logs and connector health snapshot plus Defender sensor event logs / DC System event logs for the device management / certificate‑bridge hosts [Used in NDES/Defender incidents] [Data: Sources (35); Sources (14)]. 6. WAF/load‑balancer access logs for portal FQDN and firewall audit/change logs for the maintenance weekend (templates, profile reload events, firewall rule changes) if you suspect perimeter changes - prior portal 502 and Zurich media incidents used these to prove causation [Data: Sources (15); Sources (23)]. F - If you want my immediate help - I cannot run these tenant queries or fetch your logs from the archive - the indexed records do not include your live logs. If you upload the artifacts above (or give the exact timestamps + the exported JSON/CSV for sign‑in logs, Graph membership, firewall audit, WAF logs, and connector logs) I will correlate timestamps, highlight exact offending change(s), and map them to the documented remediation steps above. (This is a recommended analysis step and is the same approach used in the cited incidents.) [Data: Sources (13); Sources (15); Sources (35); Sources (14); Sources (32)]. Bottom line / immediate classification - This is NOT a 1st‑level ticket. Classify and escalate now as second_level_support (Identity, Network, Mail, Endpoint teams). The indexed cases that match these simultaneous failures were resolved only after engineer‑level actions (ADFS/client registration alignment, WAF/template restore, full Azure AD Connect sync and group consolidation, SMTP AUTH/CA adjustment, NDES connector reinstall) [Documented precedence] [Data: Sources (11); Sources (15); Sources (13); Sources (35); Sources (32)]. If you want I will now: - Draft the exact text to paste into the escalation bridge request (which artifacts to attach and the short summary mapped to the records), or - Produce the Graph/Log query templates you should send to your Identity / Network / Mail owners to pull the items above (I will label those templates as recommended actions, not as previously executed commands in the indexed records). Which do you want (escalation note or query templates)?

question_text:
Hi IT, we updated our endpoint management security policy last Friday based on a security audit recommendation, and since then we have been seeing a chain of strange problems across the organization that all started at the same time.

First, our security dashboard now shows about 40% of our Windows laptops as 'not meeting security requirements'. Last week it was around 2-3% which is normal. The affected laptops are encrypted, the encryption keys are stored in our cloud directory like always, and nothing has changed about the laptops themselves. The flagging just says the keys are 'out of date'.

Second, several new employees who joined this week and a few people who recently received replacement laptops cannot connect to the corporate Wi-Fi at all. The corporate network just does not appear in their list of available networks. Their laptops are correctly enrolled in our management system but the Wi-Fi just is not being deployed.

Third, when users try to set up their work email on their iPhones — particularly people who recently changed phones or got new ones — the email app starts the setup, then shows a screen saying the device must be registered, then loops back to the email setup without finishing. They cannot complete the setup at all.

Fourth, we tried to push a security update for one of our internal apps via our endpoint management system, and only about 65% of devices received it. Normally we get over 95%. Same problem with a Microsoft Store app update we triggered the next day.

These all started in the same window. Some users are now bypassing the broken parts (using their personal hotspot for Wi-Fi, etc.) which is making things worse. Help us figure this out.
answer: Below I use only what the indexed incident records actually show (explicitly documented symptoms, root causes and worked remediations) and then I list recommended next actions where the records do not contain the exact artifact you asked for. Every statement that is supported by the indexed corpus is cited. I mark anything that goes beyond those records as "Recommended (not in records)". Executive summary (diagnosis from the indexed records) - The pattern you describe (many Windows laptops suddenly flagged non‑compliant; Wi‑Fi/profile not arriving; Outlook‑iOS onboarding loops; partial app rollouts) is the same sequence recorded when an Intune/BitLocker compliance rule was tightened and then evaluated against the fleet. That Intune policy change produced a large spike in "non‑compliant" device state, removed devices from the compliance‑driven dynamic group used for Wi‑Fi and app assignment, and caused App‑Protection (MAM) gating for iOS to block email setup - the documented remediation was: relax the escrow/time window, deploy an Intune remediation (re‑escrow) script, and re‑scope essential manageability assignments off the compliance dynamic group [This mapping and remediation are documented in the incident corpus] [Data: Sources (114, 10, 87, 20, 9)]. What the records explicitly document (facts you can rely on) - A tightened BitLocker / device‑compliance policy caused a large, sudden spike in non‑compliant Windows devices; remediation recorded in TI‑1700009: policy relaxed (30 days -> 365 days) and an Intune remediation script deployed to force re‑escrow; compliance returned toward baseline after that work [Data: Reports (10); Sources (114)]. - Wi‑Fi/profile and app delivery were targeted to a compliance‑based dynamic group (KF90‑Compliant‑Devices); when many devices lost compliance they were removed from that group and the Wi‑Fi/profile/app assignments evaluated as "not applicable." The operational remediation used in the records was to assign essential manageability artifacts to a static group (KF90‑Corp‑Devices / KF90‑Managed‑Devices) until compliance stabilized [Data: Sources (87); Sources (9)]. - Outlook for iOS (App Protection / MAM) onboarding loops were caused by App Protection gating on device compliance; investigators validated the symptom on sample iPhones and documented re‑enrollment / re‑add‑account or using Outlook Mobile as effective fixes for affected users until device compliance restored [Data: Sources (20); Sources (19)]. - Partial app/update rollouts were correlated to dynamic‑group membership/Intune compliance state; re‑scoping assignments and re‑running Intune deployments (after compliance correction) were recorded as the fix in similar tickets [Data: Sources (9); Sources (114)]. - In a related class of incidents, Identity/AAD Connect/provisioning problems (UPN suffix / OU filter changes) produced different but sometimes co‑occurring symptoms; those required identity team actions (revert on‑prem UPNs, update OU filter, run delta then full AAD Connect sync) - documented as the remediation when synchronization/identity mismatch was the root cause [Data: Sources (23); Sources (53); Sources (54)]. - When scheduled jobs or non‑interactive accounts were impacted by CA enforcement after sync changes, the recorded remediation included creating a service‑account exclusion group and updating the Conditional Access policy (KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA) - documented in TI‑1700004 [Data: Sources (50); Reports (5)]. Step‑by‑step resolution plan (drawn from the indexed records) Below are the actions that were actually performed and recorded as effective in prior, matching incidents. I list each step, then cite the record(s) that document that step being performed successfully. Immediate containment (stop further user workarounds and reduce risk) 1) Ask Helpdesk to block user workarounds that increase risk (temporary use of personal hotspots for corporate Wi‑Fi / data exfiltration) and route impacted users to the incident team for controlled remediation. - Rationale in records: previous incidents noted users attempting workarounds (personal tethering) that increased exposure; the runbooks and tickets show Helpdesk and Endpoint teams coordinated user communication while engineers remediated policies [Data: Sources (114, 10)]. Documented corrective actions to perform next (ordered) 2) If the root cause is an Intune BitLocker/escrow policy change: revert or relax the strict escrow‑age requirement to the interim value used in the recorded remediation (the incident record shows using 365 days as the temporary remediation) while you validate device state. Then deploy an Intune remediation script to force BitLocker re‑escrow for affected devices. Validate on samples before broad rollout. - Evidence (this exact remediation was performed and recorded): "policy revised to 365 days; Intune remediation script deployed and validated on sample devices" [Data: Reports (10); Sources (114)]. 3) For essential manageability artifacts (Wi‑Fi profile, app delivery, critical LOB configs): move those assignments off the compliance‑driven dynamic group to a static, managed device group (e.g., KF90‑Managed‑Devices / KF90‑Corp‑Devices) while device compliance is being fixed. The records show this rescoping restored Wi‑Fi/profile delivery and app rollout to the affected devices. - Evidence: the Wi‑Fi profile was reassigned to a static group and Wi‑Fi was restored in the KF90 tickets [Data: Sources (87); Sources (88)]. 4) For users with Outlook on iOS stuck in the device‑approval loop: validate/enforce device enrollment and compliance, or (as interim) advise re‑adding the account as "Microsoft Exchange" (which triggers an OAuth flow on modern iOS) or use Outlook Mobile (documented fallback). Re‑enroll the affected iOS devices in Intune where needed and validate App Protection assignment. - Evidence: investigators tested a sample iOS device and documented that removing & re‑adding the account as Microsoft Exchange or using Outlook Mobile resolved the loop; re‑enrollment in Intune was used as part of remediation [Data: Sources (20); Sources (19)]. 5) Re‑run Intune app/profile deployments (or reclone the assignments) after devices re‑enter the compliance group (or after re‑scoping assignments), then monitor completion rates. The recorded remediation reports that re‑scoping and re‑deploying fixed partial rollout issues [Data: Sources (9); Sources (114)]. 6) If any scheduled jobs / automation stopped working (non‑interactive service accounts): check whether those run‑as accounts were synchronized into Azure (became hybrid/cloud) and were evaluated by Conditional Access; if so, create a scoped exclusion group for service accounts and add the affected accounts (pattern recorded in TI‑1700004: KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA) and update the CA policy exclusion; validate Task Scheduler runs. - Evidence: service‑account outage remediated by creating KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA and updating the CA policy; AAD Connect scope correction applied as well [Data: Sources (50); Reports (5)]. Identity/sync validation (documented) 7) If the incident suggests any identity/sync contribution (e.g., UPNs changed, group/object collisions): have Identity (AAD Connect owners) inspect the AAD Connect OU filter, provisioning template used during the migration window, and run a delta then a full sync after fixes; if on‑prem UPNs were overwritten, revert the on‑prem userPrincipalName values as was done in TI‑1700001 and then run full sync to reconcile [Data: Sources (23); Sources (53); Sources (54); Reports (50)]. Validation and acceptance criteria (what the records used to close tickets) - Successful verification examples recorded in the incidents included: Intune compliance returning to baseline (e.g., ~2% non‑compliant after fix) and overnight monitoring of app/profile deployment success; Azure AD Sign‑in logs showing CA evaluation no longer blocking service accounts; Task Scheduler runs for service accounts succeeding after CA exclusion; representative users can create their Outlook on iOS account successfully after re‑enrollment or change of client [Data: Reports (10); Sources (50); Sources (114); Sources (20)]. Artifacts you should collect now (recommended, but explicitly motivated by the records) The indexed investigations used these artifacts repeatedly; the records show they were decisive in RCA. I recommend you collect and attach them to this ticket (labelled recommended because the exact export is not embedded in the indexed corpus): - Intune device compliance export for affected devices (fields: deviceId, deviceName, userUPN, lastCheckin, lastComplianceEvaluation, complianceState, nonComplianceReasons, BitLocker escrow timestamp) - used in TI‑1700009 [Data: Sources (114)]. (Recommended) - Dynamic group membership snapshot for the compliance‑based group (KF90‑Compliant‑Devices) before/after the policy change - used to see devices leaving the group [Data: Sources (87)]. (Recommended) - Azure AD Sign‑in logs for representative users/service accounts covering the policy-change window (include conditionalAccessStatus/result, failureReason, timestamp) - used to confirm CA blocks in TI‑1700004 and to validate aftermath [Data: Sources (50); Sources (97)]. (Recommended) - AAD Connect sync run history (delta/full run timestamps) and OU‑filter change record if any identity changes were made in the same window - used in UPN/OU incidents [Data: Sources (23); Sources (53)]. (Recommended) - Task Scheduler event(s) for any failed scheduled job (Event text: "Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer") if you have scheduled-task problems - this was the primary diagnostic evidence in TI‑1700004 [Data: Sources (49)]. (Recommended) Who to involve / escalation (documented ownership in the records) - Endpoint Management / Mobile Security (Endpoint owner - Employee 34 / Employee 35 in the records) for Intune exports, remediation script deployment, and device re‑enrollment actions; they led TI‑1700009 and the remediation [Data: Sources (114); Reports (10)]. - Identity / AAD Connect engineering (BU Infrastructure - Identity, Employee 30) if you find any AAD Connect / UPN / group/object sync problems; they handled the UPN reversion and OU-filter updates in the migration incidents [Data: Sources (23); Sources (53); Sources (54)]. - Security / SOC should be CC'd if users are using risky workarounds (personal hotspots), and to approve temporary CA/assignment changes as required (documented incident flows referenced Security/Consulting roles) [Data: Reports (114)]. What the indexed records do NOT contain (limitations) - The indexed records do not contain your tenant's raw logs or the exact timestamps you asked for (I cannot pull them from the corpus). They also do not contain a single, universal redaction/re‑identification policy for tickets - I therefore recommend the separate secure mapping approach if you plan automation or external sharing [Data: Sources (114); Reports (119, 14)]. Recommended (not in records) short playbook you can execute now (These are operational recommendations only - they are not verbatim, documented procedures from the indexed tickets; where the records show similar actions were effective I cite them.) A. Immediate (first 2 hours) - Tell Helpdesk to stop user workarounds and route impacted users to a central incident queue. (Recommended; records show Helpdesk/Endpoint coordinated during TI‑1700009) [Data: Sources (114)]. - Identify 3 representative sample devices/users (e.g., one Windows laptop that flipped, one new user laptop, one iPhone looping), and ask Endpoint to export Intune compliance rows for those samples for the ±48h window. (Recommended - matches how samples were used in the indexed incidents) [Data: Sources (114); Sources (20)]. B. Contain & remediate (3-24 hours) - actions that were actually performed in prior fixes - Relax the strict BitLocker escrow requirement temporarily (the incident recorded changing 30d->365d), then deploy the Intune re‑escrow remediation script; validate on samples and then expand. (Documented remediation in TI‑1700009) [Data: Reports (10); Sources (114)]. - Re‑scope critical Wi‑Fi and manageability assignments off the compliance dynamic group to a static group until device state stabilizes (documented in TI‑1700010 / TI‑1700012). (Documented) [Data: Sources (87); Sources (9)]. - For iOS Outlook loops: re‑enroll or remove & re‑add account as "Microsoft Exchange" or use Outlook Mobile as interim; validate onboarding completes (documented) [Data: Sources (20); Sources (19)]. C. Identity checks (if artifacts indicate sync/UPN issues) - Ask Identity team to check AAD Connect OU/filter and provisioning template used during the prior migration wave; if on‑prem UPNs were overwritten revert them and run a full sync (documented in TI‑1700001/TI‑1700002). (Documented) [Data: Sources (23); Sources (53); Sources (54)]. D. Validate & close (24-72 hours) - Verify Intune compliance returns to baseline and the dynamic group has re‑populated; verify Wi‑Fi/profile assignments apply; verify Outlook iOS onboarding succeeds for the sample users; monitor app rollout completion metrics overnight. The records show these validation steps were used to close tickets [Data: Reports (10); Sources (114); Sources (9)]. Suggested contents to attach to this ticket now (recommended) - Small Intune compliance CSV for 3 sample devices (fields per above). - One exported Sign‑in log row for a sample service account/device showing conditionalAccessStatus in the ±5m clinic for an observed failure (redacted IP if needed). - If you have them, the AAD Connect run timestamps for any delta/full syncs that occurred in the window. (These are recommended and match the artifacts that previous teams used; the records do not contain your tenant logs) [Data: Sources (50, 114, 23)]. Final note on support tier and next owner - Based on the indexed cases that match this incident, this is a second_level_support issue requiring coordination between Endpoint (Intune) and Identity (AAD Connect) engineering teams. In prior incidents Employee 34 (Endpoint) and Employee 30 (Identity) executed the same cross‑team remediation steps [Data: Sources (114); Sources (23); Reports (10, 50)]. If you want I will: - Produce the exact copy‑paste request you can send to your Endpoint team to pull the Intune export (I prepared a draft earlier and can finalize it with the precise timestamps you provide). (Recommended) - Or: produce the exact Microsoft Graph / Entra UI steps to extract the sign‑in + compliance rows for the windows/iOS sample pairs you choose (recommended if you have a Global Admin to run it). Which do you want me to prepare now - (A) the ready‑to‑send export request to Endpoint (CSV header + wording), or (B) the Graph/portal extraction steps?

question_text:
Hi IT, our team did some kind of internal infrastructure maintenance over a weekend a couple of weeks ago — something behind the scenes that wasn't supposed to affect anyone visibly. Since then we have been getting a series of reports from different parts of the company that look unrelated but all started around the same time.

First, when people try to open our internal HR system in their browsers — to look at their records or report sick days — their browsers throw a big warning page saying they cannot trust the connection. They have to click through some 'advanced' option to actually get to the page. Same thing on a couple of other internal websites that everyone normally uses.

Second, our remote workers who work from home or while travelling — about 80 people — say they cannot get their secure work-from-home connection established anymore. Their connection software just refuses to authenticate. The error mentions something about not being able to verify the server identity. They were working fine until this maintenance work happened, and nothing changed on their laptops.

Third, the leadership team uses a special configuration for their email correspondence so that confidential communications can be cryptographically verified — outgoing messages they send carry a kind of seal, and they can read sealed messages they receive from external partners. That whole feature stopped working. They cannot apply the seal to outgoing email anymore, and they cannot read sealed email they receive. They were given new credentials around the time of the maintenance work, but those credentials do not seem to do anything.

Fourth, several of our internal business applications — the HR app, our SharePoint installation, and our network access control system — report that they can no longer reach our central user-account system through the protected channel they normally use. They CAN reach it through the unprotected channel, but our security policy does not allow operating that way.

These all started in the same window. Different teams are pointing at each other — the infrastructure team that did the maintenance, the application owners, the security team. We need an actual answer about what is happening.
answer: Below I use only what the indexed incident corpus documents, and I separate (A) facts the records explicitly show from (B) recommended / procedural actions that go beyond the indexed text. Where a record supports a statement I cite it inline. Executive summary (short) - The indexed incidents that match your four symptoms were caused by PKI / certificate lifecycle and certificate‑template changes during a weekend maintenance: (a) an issuing CA / intermediate that was renewed but not published to the internal AIA/CRL distribution point (causing browser chain/trust warnings for internal HTTPS sites), (b) a Domain‑Controller certificate‑template change that removed a required EKU, which led to LDAPS/TLS handshake failures for applications, (c) a VPN certificate/template that lacked the IPsec/IKE EKU causing IKEv2 mutual‑auth failures for remote users, and (d) auto‑renewal of S/MIME certificates where private keys were not present on endpoints, requiring CA key‑archive recovery and PFX re‑import for executives. Those specific remediations (publish issuing CA to AIA, revert template and re‑enroll DC certs, fix VPN template and reissue, recover private keys and import PFXs) are recorded as the actions that restored service in prior cases. [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55); Reports (210); Reports (575); Reports (36)] A. What the indexed records explicitly document (mapping your four symptom classes to recorded root causes and fixes) 1) Browser trust warnings for internal HR and other sites - Recorded cause: an issuing‑CA renewal occurred and the renewed issuing/intermediate certificate was not initially published to the internal AIA/CRL distribution point; clients could not build the chain and showed "issuer's certificate not found" / NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID until the issuing cert was published and clients refreshed. [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)] 2) Remote VPN clients (≈80 users) cannot establish secure server‑auth because server identity cannot be verified - Recorded cause: VPN/CheckPoint IKEv2 failures traced to a certificate template used for the VPN/server cert that omitted the required IPsec/IKE EKU. The appliance logs showed chain/validation / EKU mismatch messages and remediation required re‑issuing a corrected cert/template on the VPN device. [Data: Reports (575); Sources (104)] 3) Leadership S/MIME signing/decryption stopped working - Recorded cause: S/MIME certificates were auto‑renewed but private keys were not available on endpoints (the CA had the keys in its key‑archive). The PKI team recovered archived private keys, exported them as password‑protected PFX, delivered them via a secure channel and had executives import the PFX files to restore S/MIME signing and decryption. [Data: Reports (36); Entities (577)] 4) Internal apps (HR, SharePoint, NAC) cannot connect via LDAPS/TLS but can use unprotected LDAP - Recorded cause: a Domain‑Controller certificate template change removed the Kerberos / DC EKU or otherwise produced DC certs incompatible with LDAPS consumers; auto‑enrollment caused DCs to pick up incompatible certs, which produced LDAPS/TLS handshake failures until the template was corrected and DCs re‑enrolled (certutil -pulse was used in prior remediations to accelerate re‑enrollment). [Data: Reports (210); Reports (37)] B. Step‑by‑step resolution based on documented prior cases Below are the concrete remediation steps that were actually performed in prior incidents (I mark each step with the supporting record(s)). These are the actions you should follow in the same order, and you should escalate to your PKI/Identity/Network owners immediately. Step 1 - Triage & hold change window - Action (documented pattern): Open an escalated PKI/Identity incident and assign PKI owner + Identity owner (prior incidents used BU PKI + Employee‑level identity owners). Do not make further certificate/template changes until the remediation sequence is complete. Evidence: the incidents show PKI/Identity teams were engaged and owned the remediations [Data: Reports (574); Reports (36)]. Step 2 - Publish the renewed issuing/intermediate CA to the internal AIA/CRL distribution point (fix browser chain) - Action (documented): Ensure the issuing/intermediate certificate(s) from the weekend renewal are published to the internal AIA/CRL locations and that those URLs are reachable from client networks; verify HTTP GET of the AIA URL succeeds and clients can fetch the intermediate. Evidence (same remediation done in recorded incident): publishing the issuing CA to AIA removed browser chain errors for HR portal/SharePoint [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)]. Step 3 - Validate and refresh client trust - Action (documented): On representative client machines, validate the chain (browser certificate inspector or certutil -verify) and force certificate store refresh / group policy update on sample endpoints (previous incidents noted using gpupdate/certutil -pulse where applicable) until clients stop showing trust warnings. Evidence: record shows client refresh plus AIA publish removed the browser warning in the prior case [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)]. Step 4 - Revert incorrect Domain‑Controller template and re‑enroll DC certificates - Action (documented): If a DC template was changed producing certificates without required EKUs (e.g., missing Kerberos / DC EKU), revert the template to the correct settings, remove or replace the incompatible DC certs from the DC personal store, and force re‑enrollment on each DC (certutil -pulse). Validate LDAPS binds from affected applications. Evidence: the prior incident recorded a DC template removal of the Kerberos EKU, forced DC re‑enrollment, and subsequent validation of LDAPS functionality [Data: Reports (210); Reports (37)]. Step 5 - Fix VPN/server certificate template and reissue VPN server cert - Action (documented): Update the VPN/server certificate template to include the required IPsec/IKE EKU and reissue the corrected certificate to the VPN concentrator(s). Validate IKEv2 tunnels with a test user and confirm remote users can authenticate. Evidence: prior CheckPoint IKEv2 outages were resolved by correcting the missing IPsec/IKE EKU on the VPN cert template and reissuing the certificate on the appliance [Data: Reports (575); Sources (104)]. Step 6 - Recover archived S/MIME private keys and restore executive mail signing/decryption - Action (documented): For executives affected, use the CA key‑archive recovery procedure to extract the private key for each user, export as password‑protected PFX, deliver via a secure channel, and have the user import the PFX into their Personal store. Validate S/MIME signing and decryption after import. (Do this under PKI owner + InfoSec oversight). Evidence: prior ticket shows exactly this recovery/export/import procedure restored executive S/MIME functionality [Data: Reports (36); Entities (577)]. Step 7 - Post‑fix validation and monitoring - Action (documented): After each change above, validate: (a) browser access to HR/SharePoint with sample users, (b) LDAPS binds from HR app / NAC / SharePoint and confirm they use TLS, (c) VPN connectivity for a cohort of remote users, (d) S/MIME signing/decryption for executives, and (e) capture sign‑in / application logs showing success. Monitor for 24-72 hours. The prior incidents recorded explicit overnight validation and monitoring before ticket closure. Evidence: prior tickets recorded overnight validation and closure after the steps above showed success [Data: Reports (574); Reports (36); Reports (37)]. C. Artifacts you must provide (the indexed records show teams collected these during triage) The indexed corpus shows investigators required specific logs/artifacts to confirm root cause and validate remediation. The records do not contain these artifacts for your environment - you must collect and provide them. (These are recommended evidence‑collection items; the records show the same classes were used in past investigations.) Collect and attach: - CA server event logs and CA audit entries around the weekend maintenance (AIA/CRL publish events and template edits). [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)] - The VPN concentrator / IKE logs that show "client cert chain validation failed" / "EKU mismatch" for the affected user sessions. [Data: Reports (575); Sources (104)] - A representative browser certificate chain screenshot from the HR site showing the chain error ("Issuer's certificate not found"). [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)] - On one or more Domain Controllers: certutil -store MY output and the System/Application event entries showing auto‑enrollment/cert issuance around the weekend (to confirm the DC cert contents and EKUs). [Data: Reports (210); Reports (37)] - Samples of application logs (HR app, SharePoint, NAC) showing LDAPS/TLS handshake failure messages and timestamps. [Data: Reports (210)] - For S/MIME: CA key‑archive audit rows showing recovered keys and PFX export events (if available), and a validated import confirmation from an executive (screenshot or mail test). [Data: Reports (36)] D. What the indexed knowledge does not contain (so I cannot complete the fix from here) - The indexed corpus documents the *process* and prior successful remediations but does not include your tenant/server logs, the CA event log entries, the actual certificate files, or the VPN/IKE traces needed to declare the incident fully closed in your environment. I cannot perform the publish, template reversion, DC re‑enroll or PFX exports for you from the indexed records - those must be executed by your PKI and Identity operators with the artifacts above. [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (36); Reports (575)] E. Immediate recommended actions to start now (these are operational recommendations, marked recommended) - Recommendation: Escalate this ticket to PKI (CA owner), Identity/AD team, and Network/VPN owner immediately and provide the artifact list from Section C. Prior cases were handled by these teams and required cross‑team coordination. [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (575)] - Recommendation: Do not re‑enable any maintenance automation that may reapply templates or reissue certs until you have a controlled runbook (the incidents recommend adding a preflight AIA/CRL verification gate into the runbook). [Data: Reports (574)] - Recommendation: For executives' PFX delivery, use a secure channel and passworded PFX files per the prior S/MIME remediation. [Data: Reports (36)] F. Short, actionable handoff you can copy into the ticket now - Escalation owners to add: PKI owner (on‑call), Identity/AD (owner), Network/VPN owner, and Application owners (HR, SharePoint, NAC). Cite this ticket as "possible PKI / template / AIA publish regression - symptoms: browser CA errors, VPN IKEv2 EKU failures, S/MIME private‑key missing for executives, LDAPS handshake failures." [Data: Reports (574); Reports (575); Reports (36); Reports (210)] - Ask PKI to: (a) confirm whether a CA/issuing/intermediate renewal occurred on the weekend and whether the issuing cert was published to AIA/CRL; (b) if not published, publish the CA cert to AIA and confirm HTTP reachability. (Refer to the prior incident where publishing fixed browser errors.) [Data: Reports (574); Sources (55)] - Ask Identity/AD to: (a) confirm whether DC certificate template edits were applied and whether DCs auto‑enrolled incompatible certs; (b) if a template change caused the issue, revert template and force DC re‑enrollment (certutil -pulse) and validate LDAPS. [Data: Reports (210); Reports (37)] - Ask Network/VPN owner to: (a) check concentrator logs for IKE/cert chain/EKU errors; (b) if template lacked IPsec/IKE EKU, reissue VPN server cert from corrected template and re-deploy. [Data: Reports (575); Sources (104)] - Ask PKI to perform executive key recovery and secure PFX delivery for any executives who cannot sign/decrypt; validate import and S/MIME end‑to‑end. [Data: Reports (36)] G. Classification and rationale - Based on the indexed cases with the same multi‑symptom pattern, classify this as second_level_support (requires PKI/Identity/Network engineering escalation). The prior incidents with identical symptoms required engineer‑level template and CA operations, full trust‑chain fixes, and coordinated re‑enrollment and were handled by PKI/Identity owners rather than first‑line support. [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (575); Reports (36)] H. If you want, I will prepare next artifacts for the responders - I can draft the exact log/export request text to send to your CA operator, DC owners, VPN owner and Exchange/PKI team (e.g., exact event logs, certutil outputs, IKE trace snippets and sample sign‑in timestamps). This is a recommended action (not taken verbatim from prior tickets) but will mirror the artifacts used in the recorded remediations. Tell me which team(s) you want me to prepare the request for and I will produce those short templates. Closing / one‑line summary - The indexed evidence maps your four concurrent symptom classes to the documented PKI / certificate‑template / AIA publication failures that occurred in previous weekend maintenance windows. The recorded fix sequence was: publish issuing CA to AIA, revert erroneous template(s) and force DC re‑enrollment, correct VPN template and reissue VPN cert, and recover S/MIME private keys from CA archive and import PFXs - each step is recorded as having restored services in prior incidents [Data: Reports (574); Reports (37); Reports (575); Reports (36)]. To complete this for your environment we need the artifacts listed in Section C and the PKI/Identity/Network teams to perform the documented remediation steps. If you want those ready‑to‑send artifact‑request messages (one per team), say which teams to include and I will produce them marked as recommended next steps (not as items directly executed in the records).

question_text:
Hi IT-Team, sorry but we have a very odd combination of issues since yesterday and multiple teams are blocked.

First: In D365 (Finance & Operations), Financial Reports is basically unusable. When we go to General Ledger -> Financial Reports -> Edit, it either errors or just spins forever. It is not only one person: several users report it. Also, older reports suddenly show the author as "unknown", and when we try to open or create a report it sometimes says the report cannot be found. This is happening in test and production, so we cannot even use test as a workaround.

Second: our new hotline numbers from the current marketing campaign are dead. Calls to the old main number still ring through, but if a customer calls one of the new ones it fails right away. The carrier insists the calls reach our edge, but they never seem to land with anyone here.

Third: the Basel office has a very specific behavior on outside calls. The call connects, people can talk briefly, and then it cuts off after roughly half a minute almost every time, like clockwork. Internal calls are fine, and colleagues at other sites are fine. Apparently something on our voice setup was changed last week.

Fourth: a few team leads say they no longer get any kind of pop-up or alert when Teams messages come in on their laptops. Messages still arrive — they just only notice them once they actually open Teams. Their phones are unaffected. Windows updates ran last week and at least one person had some focus mode turned on during a meeting.

Is this connected or just multiple incidents at once? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Below I use only what the indexed incident corpus records, and I clearly separate (A) what the records explicitly document about similar failures and prior remediations and (B) what I recommend next for this ticket when the records do not contain the needed live artifacts. For each factual statement I cite the most relevant indexed records. TL;DR - classification and immediate action - Based on the indexed records, this is not a routine single‑user fault. The mix of cross‑service symptoms (D365 Financial Reports spanning UAT+PROD, Teams SIP/Direct‑Routing/SBC behavior, firewall/NAT timing, and client notification issues) matches prior incidents that were escalated to engineering/identity/UC/Network teams. Recommended classification: second_level_support - escalate to Application (AOS/IIS/FRDB) owners, BU Infrastructure - Identity, Unified Communications (SBC/Direct Routing) owners, and Network/Firewall owners for coordinated investigation [Data: Sources (0); Sources (13); Sources (16)]. A. What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence you can rely on) 1) D365 / Financial Reports - Similar outages required AOS/IIS and Data‑Mart investigation, vendor escalation and (in prior cases) a Data‑Mart reset or AOS/IIS/TLS configuration changes. The Management‑Reporting incident recorded vendor escalation and changes to AOS/IIS/TLS + Data‑Mart actions as the remediation path [Data: Sources (0); Sources (56)]. 2) New hotline numbers (calls reach carrier -> edge but not users) - Prior cases where new number ranges failed at the carrier->edge showed the SBC translation/normalization table was missing entries; a temporary translation rule on the SBC validated the fix, then a permanent translation/routing update was applied [Data: Sources (8)]. 3) Basel ~30‑second call drops - Prior Basel incidents reproduced an SBC BYE at ~29-31s and traced root cause to NAT/session timer / UDP timeout mismatches or firewall/NAT behavior on the Voice VLAN; remedy was aligning NAT/session timeouts and SBC/session‑timer parameters (and firewall port rules where Media Bypass is used) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. 4) Teams desktop notifications / no pop‑up banners - Prior endpoint cases documenting missing Teams pop‑ups were resolved by checking Windows notification settings and Focus Assist, confirming Teams client notification permissions, and running Teams client diagnostics / Test Call. Windows updates and Focus/Do‑Not‑Disturb states were frequent contributors to missing desktop pop‑ups [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19); Sources (37)]. 5) Escalation pattern for multi‑system incidents - The indexed runbook/incident summaries show the canonical escalation path for multi‑service outages: CRM/portal intake -> BU Customer Services Run / Helpdesk triage -> escalate to Core Engineering / BU Infrastructure (Identity / UC / Network) when cross‑system or host/VM/dir‑sync impacts are present. Prior similar multi‑domain incidents were treated as second‑level and required coordinated cross‑team fixes [Data: Sources (3); Sources (34); Sources (13)]. B. Concrete, step‑by‑step resolution plan (mix of record‑backed steps and recommended actions) Below: for each step I indicate whether it is explicitly documented in the indexed records (Evidence) or whether it is a recommendation (Recommended) because the records don't include the live artifacts. Phase 0 - immediate triage (all four symptoms) - Action 0.1 - Gather and publish scope and timestamps now (Evidence + Recommended) - Collect: list of affected users (samples per symptom), first‑observed timestamp for each symptom (local & UTC if available), and whether the failures reproduce from multiple locations/browsers. Prior records show collecting this scope is standard triage and speeds escalation [Data: Sources (3); Sources (34)]. (Recommended - the records show teams always gathered scope.) - Action 0.2 - Open a cross‑functional bridge and assign owners (Evidence + Recommended) - Immediately open an escalated incident bridging Application (AOS/FRDB), Identity, Unified Communications (SBC/Direct Routing), and Network/Firewall teams and assign a single incident lead (Core Engineering / System Engineer). The indexed runbook explicitly recommends cross‑team bridgecalls for cross‑system outages [Data: Sources (3); Sources (34)]. (Recommended) Phase 1 - D365 Financial Reports (edit/view errors, authors = "unknown", "report not found") - What the records show worked previously (Evidence) - Prior Management‑Reporting / Financial‑Reports outages were resolved by investigating AOS/IIS/TLS configuration, Data‑Mart/FRDB service health and - when required - performing a Data‑Mart reset or AOS/IIS remediation and escalating to vendor/Microsoft if needed [Data: Sources (0); Sources (55)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended; map to prior-case actions) 1. Collect: - Error screenshots and full browser URL for a failing Edit action (several users). - Server‑side logs: AOS app logs, IIS logs for the Financial Reports site, and FRDB/Data‑Mart logs covering the primary incident window. (Recommended - prior tickets used exactly these artifacts) [Data: Sources (0)]. 2. Confirm whether any recent OS/patch/CLR/IISCRYPTO changes or TLS/cipher changes were applied to AOS/IIS in the 24-48 hours prior (Evidence: past incident required TLS/IIS changes) [Data: Sources (0)]. 3. Check whether "author = unknown" corresponds to any directory/group/object changes (Evidence + Recommended): export Azure AD audit log entries for group/object changes and SharePoint/D365 author mappings in the same window - previous incidents tied objectID/group changes to authorship/visibility anomalies [Data: Sources (13); Sources (30)]. 4. If AOS/IIS or FRDB errors point to service instability, escalate immediately to Application/Core Engineering and consider vendor/Microsoft escalation per precedent. (Evidence - prior incidents used vendor escalation) [Data: Sources (0)]. Phase 2 - Hotline numbers fail (carrier sees delivery to edge; users never ring) - What records show worked previously (Evidence) - Missing SBC translation entries for new ranges produced INVITE -> 404 at the SBC; temporary translation rules validated the fix and permanent translation/routing table updates were applied. SBC + carrier traces were decisive evidence [Data: Sources (8)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended) 1. Collect from the carrier and SBC (Recommended): - Sample failed number(s), exact UTC timestamp(s) (caller, callee), and carrier trace IDs. - SBC SIP trace for those timestamps (full INVITE/response sequences) and the SBC translation table snapshot (pre/post) and any temporary translation-rule text applied during prior tests. (Recommended - this parallels TI‑1612036 approach) [Data: Sources (8)]. 2. Have UC/SBC engineer check SBC translation/normalization entries for those number ranges and apply a temporary translation rule to reproduce and validate routing - prior incident applied a temporary rule and then a permanent update [Data: Sources (8)]. (Recommended / Evidence) 3. Correlate with Teams Admin Center/voice provisioning telemetry to confirm whether the tenant assigned any voice policy provisioning changes to the affected users (Evidence: provisioning-script misassignment caused other voice outages) [Data: Sources (9)]. (Recommended) Phase 3 - Basel ~30s call drops (one‑way/short disconnect at ~29-31s) - What records show worked previously (Evidence) - SBC logs showed SBC-originated BYE at ~29-31s; diagnosis traced this to NAT/UDP session timeouts on Voice VLAN or session‑timer mismatch. Fix used NAT timeout alignment and SBC/session‑timer changes (Basel case) or firewall port adjustments (Zurich case) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended) 1. Collect: - Representative failed call Call‑ID(s) and full SBC SIP trace (INVITE -> 200 OK -> ACK -> BYE) with exact timestamps. (Recommended - prior incidents used these) [Data: Sources (16)]. - Firewall/NAT logs for the client side and Voice VLAN, showing NAT session creation/expiry and UDP session timeout values for the same timestamps. (Recommended) [Data: Sources (16)]. 2. Correlate the SBC BYE timestamp with any NAT session expiry or firewall deny at that same time. If NAT session timeout is ~30s, align to Direct Routing blueprint (increase to match SBC session timers; prior fixes changed NAT timers or SBC timers to remove 30s drops) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Recommended / Evidence) 3. If Media Bypass is enabled at the site, verify outbound firewall dynamic RTP range allowed; Zurich examples show restricting dynamic RTP to only 3478-3481 caused audio issues until corrected [Data: Sources (23)]. (Recommended / Evidence) Phase 4 - Teams desktop pop‑ups missing (messages arrive only when Teams opened) - What records show worked previously (Evidence) - Prior Teams client notification issues resolved by verifying Windows Focus Assist / Do Not Disturb, Teams notification settings, Teams Test Call, and Teams client updates. A recorded case found Focus Assist or app permission settings to be the cause and resolved locally [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19); Sources (37)]. (Evidence) - Immediate steps to execute now (recommended) 1. On affected laptops: confirm Windows Focus Assist / Do Not Disturb is off and that Teams has Notifications permission; capture a Teams client log and run a Teams Test Call / Notification test and record timestamp. (Recommended - prior tickets used these checks) [Data: Sources (37); Sources (19); Sources (42)]. 2. If many users were updated last week, check whether a Windows Update or Teams client upgrade coincided with the symptom; if so, collect client update timestamp(s) and consider rolling back or reapplying the Teams client fix per vendor guidance. (Recommended) C. Correlation: single root cause vs multiple co‑incidents - what the records say - The indexed corpus shows that when multiple independent subsystems (identity/group mappings, provisioning scripts, Direct Routing/SBC, print servers) fail after an org‑chart/group change, the pattern commonly indicates a provisioning/directory sync ripple that affects many downstream services and justifies second‑level escalation and cross‑team remediation [Data: Sources (13); Sources (9); Sources (16)]. (Evidence) - Therefore: because you have cross‑service impact (D365 + telephony + site‑specific UC + Teams clients), the pattern aligns with prior cross‑system incidents that were escalated to second_level_support and required coordinated engineering work [Data: Sources (13); Sources (3)]. (Evidence -> classification recommendation) D. Concrete owners and who to notify now (recommended; aligned to past practice) - Immediately notify and assemble: - Application / D365 owner + Core Engineering (AOS/IIS/FRDB) - per Management Reports precedent [Data: Sources (0)]. (Recommended) - BU Infrastructure - Identity (Azure AD / Azure AD Connect / provisioning) - to review group/object collisions and provisioning script effects [Data: Sources (13); Sources (9)]. (Recommended) - Unified Communications / SBC owner - request SBC SIP traces and translation table review [Data: Sources (8)]. (Recommended) - Network / Firewall team - request NAT/session timeout values and firewall change history; check Voice VLAN NAT policies and RTP port rules [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Recommended) - Endpoint/Desktop support - run Teams client/Focus Assist checks and capture client logs for Teams pop‑up issue [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19)]. (Recommended) E. Minimum evidence to attach to the incident ticket (so 2nd‑level teams can act immediately) - D365: AOS IIS logs (primary window), FRDB/DataMart error logs, screenshots of the error, and the list of affected users and exact timestamps [Data: Sources (0)]. (Recommended) - Telephony (hotline): sample called number(s), UTC timestamps, carrier trace IDs, and SBC SIP trace(s) for the same times, plus SBC translation table export and Teams Admin Center policy assignment snapshots [Data: Sources (8); Sources (9)]. (Recommended) - Basel UC: SBC SIP traces showing BYE at ~29-31s, firewall/NAT logs for the Voice VLAN showing UDP/NAT session timeouts, and the site change ticket that moved the network segment (if any) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]. (Recommended) - Teams endpoint: Teams client logs and Windows Event logs for Focus Assist/Notifications around the sample test time; list of affected hostnames and exact update timestamps [Data: Sources (42); Sources (19)]. (Recommended) F. Final classification (explicit) - Classify this ticket as second_level_support (escalate to engineering/identity/UC/network). Rationale: prior indexed incidents with the same multi‑domain scope were escalated and required hands‑on engineering (Azure AD Connect / provisioning fixes; SBC/firewall parameter changes; AOS/IIS/Data Mart actions) rather than first‑line fixes [Data: Sources (13); Sources (9); Sources (0); Sources (16)]. (Evidence) G. If the indexed records do NOT contain enough information to fully resolve the ticket - statement - The indexed records document the workflows, past root causes and the artifacts investigators used, but they do NOT contain your tenant's live logs (AOS/IIS/FRDB error logs, SBC SIP traces, firewall/NAT logs, Azure AD sign‑in logs, or actual provisioning CI job runs). To resolve this incident now we must collect and attach the live artifacts listed above. The prior incidents show those exact artifacts were decisive [Data: Sources (0); Sources (8); Sources (16); Sources (13)]. (Explicit statement) H. Suggested immediate message to paste into your ticket (copy‑paste - recommended) - "Escalate to second_level_support: Application (AOS/IIS/FRDB), Identity (Azure AD / Azure AD Connect / Provisioning), Unified Communications (SBC/Direct Routing), and Network/Firewall required. Critical artifacts requested: (1) AOS/IIS+FRDB logs for 48h window around failure; (2) sample failed hotline numbers + carrier traceIDs + SBC SIP traces and SBC translation table; (3) SBC SIP traces + firewall NAT/session logs for Basel sample calls showing BYE ~29-31s; (4) Teams client logs + Windows Event logs and Focus Assist state for affected hosts. Please attach these artifacts and confirm ownership for each scope so we can start cross‑correlation. Classification: second_level_support. [References: similar prior resolutions: Financial Reports/AOS remediation (TI‑722511) [Data: Sources (0)]; SBC translation-rule remediation (TI‑1612036) [Data: Sources (8)]; Basel NAT/session timeout BYE ~29-31s (TI‑1604879) [Data: Sources (16); Sources (23)]; SharePoint/group/Entra remediation (TI‑1583327) [Data: Sources (13)].]" (Recommended) - End - If you want I will: - Draft the exact log export filters and filenames to hand to each owner (AOS/IIS, SBC, Firewall, Azure AD Connect, Teams client). (Recommended - I will produce them on request.) - Or, if you can attach one concrete artifact now (one SBC SIP trace or the AOS IIS error log for the Financial Reports error), I will analyze and map it to the most likely remediation path shown in the indexed incidents.

question_text:
Hi IT, we are a couple of weeks into our migration from our old internal sign-in system to the new cloud-based one. The first batch of users went through smoothly, but since we did the second batch about two weeks ago, we have been getting a series of strange complaints from those users that we cannot make sense of.

First, several users from the second batch say they can sign in to their cloud email and Teams without any problem, but when they try to open our internal procurement system or our document management system (which run on our internal servers), they get an error saying their account is not recognized. The first-batch users do not have this issue.

Second, we are seeing strange inconsistencies with security groups. A user might be correctly in the new combined group on the internal directory side, but in the cloud they still show as a member of an old group. This is breaking some access permissions because the cloud and internal systems read different group memberships for the same person.

Third, the same affected users are saying their mobile email apps (built-in iPhone Mail and the Android Gmail app) keep asking for password and never connect. Outlook on the phone still works fine for them but the built-in mail apps just loop on the password prompt.

Fourth — and this only came up because someone noticed overnight reports were missing — several scheduled tasks that run on our internal servers using service accounts have started failing with authentication errors. The service accounts have not been touched by the migration as far as we know, but the failures started right around the time the second batch was migrated.

These all started in the same window but they look like four completely different problems. We need to figure out what is going on and how to fix it.
answer: Below I separate (A) what the indexed incident records explicitly document about the four symptom classes you reported, and then (B) a concrete, step‑by‑step remediation plan that matches the documented fixes used in prior incidents. Where a step is taken from the recorded incidents I mark it "(documented)" and cite the ticket(s). Where I propose an action that is not present as an executed item in the indexed records I mark it "(recommended)". If something required to reach a full technical conclusion is missing from the indexed records (for example raw AAD Connect sync logs, exported CA policy JSON, Task Scheduler .evtx exports), I call that out and show which artifacts to collect. A. What the indexed records explicitly document (short summary) - UPN / provisioning writeback in wave‑2: - In a prior second‑wave incident investigators found a provisioning/template error that wrote the new cloud UPN suffix back into on‑prem userPrincipalName attributes. That produced exactly the symptom you describe: cloud sign‑in OK, on‑prem apps (that match by UPN) report "user not found" or "account not recognized." Remediation recorded: revert on‑prem UPNs, then run AAD Connect delta + full sync and validate access [documented in TI‑1700001] [Data: Sources (22)]. - OU / AAD Connect sync‑scope omission and group divergence: - Investigations documented cases where groups were created inside a Migration_Phase2 OU that was initially excluded from AAD Connect; cloud continued to show the old group object while on‑prem showed the new group. Remediation recorded: update AAD Connect OU filter, run delta then full sync, and reconcile memberships [documented in TI‑1700002] [Data: Sources (53)]. - Mobile built‑in mail clients looping (EAS / Basic Auth vs Modern Auth): - Prior incidents show built‑in iOS/Android Mail clients using Exchange ActiveSync with Basic Auth were blocked when tenant enforcement required Modern Authentication; Outlook Mobile (OAuth) kept working. Remediation used: force clients to re‑enroll/re‑add to trigger OAuth or use Outlook Mobile / re‑enroll in Intune [documented in KF70 mobile incidents] [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. - Scheduled tasks / service‑accounts failing due to Conditional Access after sync: - Tickets record scheduled‑task failures (Task Scheduler error "Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer.") after service accounts were moved into an OU included by AAD Connect and thus subject to an interactive‑MFA Conditional Access policy. Remediation recorded: correct AAD Connect filter/scope *and* create a dedicated CA exclusion group for non‑interactive service accounts (named KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA in the case record), update the CA policy to exclude it, then validate scheduled tasks. This sequence resolved the scheduled‑task outages in the recorded incident [documented in TI‑1700004] [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. B. Step‑by‑step resolution plan mapped to documented fixes Note: the steps below are ordered and combine (1) immediate triage, (2) identity/sync remediation, (3) CA / service‑account fix, (4) client remediation (mobile), and (5) validation and runbook updates. Each step shows whether it is explicitly documented in the indexed records (and cites those records), or whether it is a recommended next action not itself contained as an executed item in the records. Immediate triage and handoff (do first) 1) Escalate to Identity / AAD Connect engineering (second level). Rationale: similar cases in the indexed corpus required BU Infrastructure - Identity involvement (Employee 30) because the fix touches Azure AD Connect, on‑prem AD attributes, and Conditional Access [documented]. Action: open an engineering handoff and include this case's sample UPN(s), the migration wave timestamp, and the four symptom classes [documented mapping to prior cases] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Sources (49)]. 2) Collect and attach the minimal evidence package for the engineers (recommended - not all artifacts are present in the indexed corpus): sample affected UPNs (1-3), AAD Connect sync run history for the migration window (delta/full job timestamps), current AAD Connect OU filter config (which OUs are IN/OUT), Azure AD Sign‑in log entries for affected UPNs (time window), Task Scheduler event log extracts from servers where scheduled tasks failed (the error lines & timestamps), and a copy/list of Conditional Access policies (or screenshots/JSON export for the policy that looks like the tenant baseline / KF70‑Require‑MFA). Prior incidents used these exact artifacts to diagnose and fix the problem [recommended; the records show these items were requested/used but do not contain the raw exports themselves] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (54); Sources (49); Sources (50)]. Identity / AAD Connect diagnosis and fix 3) Confirm whether on‑prem userPrincipalName attributes were overwritten for the affected users (diagnostic step used in TI‑1700001) - compare on‑prem UPN for sample users to the Entra/Azure AD UPNs and the object's source/immutableId. If the on‑prem UPN was written to the new suffix, revert the on‑prem userPrincipalName to the original value [documented in TI‑1700001]. After reversion run an AAD Connect sync: initial delta to confirm changes then a full sync to ensure propagation. Validate access for the sample users to on‑prem apps [documented remediation] [Data: Sources (22)]. 4) Inspect AAD Connect OU filter / sync scope for the Migration_Phase2 OU and for the OU(s) where service accounts or newly created groups were placed. If Migration_Phase2 or a service‑accounts OU was accidentally excluded or later included incorrectly, adjust the OU filter according to the intended design, then run delta/full syncs (documented pattern). Use Graph/portal membership exports to compare cloud vs on‑prem group objectIDs and membership lists to detect displayName vs ObjectId collisions and to reconcile membership differences [documented diagnostics and remediation used in TI‑1700002 / group sync incidents] [Data: Sources (53); Reports (209); Sources (54)]. Conditional Access / service‑account handling 5) Check whether service accounts are now synchronized to Entra ID (check user "source" attribute). If they are synchronized and you observe scheduled‑task failures with Task Scheduler logon errors at the same timestamps as Azure AD sign‑in log CA failures (e.g., "Failed - Conditional Access requires MFA"), this is exactly the signature recorded in TI‑1700004 [documented]. If so, apply the documented remediation: create a dedicated exclusion group for non‑interactive service accounts (example name used in the record: KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA), add the impacted service accounts to it, and update the Conditional Access policy to exclude that group (so non‑interactive jobs are not forced to do interactive MFA). Validate scheduled tasks run successfully afterward [documented remediation] [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. Notes and caveats: the indexed records show the exclusion group KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA was created and used successfully in TI‑1700004, but they do not contain a tenant export of the full CA policy JSON or a time‑stamped membership CSV for the exclusion group - you must export/capture those artifacts in your tenant as evidence [Data: Sources (50); Entities (755)]. (See "Artifacts you must collect" below.) Mobile client remediation (built‑in mail apps) 6) For the users who see password loops in built‑in Mail apps: the documented pattern shows those clients were attempting legacy Basic Auth (EAS) and being blocked by tenant Modern Authentication enforcement; Outlook Mobile (OAuth) continued to work [documented]. Remediation recorded: on affected devices, remove and re‑add the account as "Microsoft Exchange" (to trigger OAuth) or enroll/re‑enroll devices into Intune / App Protection as required; where the native client cannot be moved to OAuth, use Outlook Mobile as a supported fallback. Validate mailflow on a sample device before advising all users [documented fixes in KF70 mobile incidents] [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. Validation and closure 7) Validate with concrete evidence (documented practice): for sample users confirm (a) cloud sign‑in (successful), (b) on‑prem app sign‑in (successful), (c) the cloud and on‑prem group memberships now match (Graph export diff), (d) mobile client connects properly (OAuth / Outlook Mobile), and (e) scheduled tasks run successfully overnight for affected service accounts. Record the sign‑in log IDs and Task Scheduler event IDs used for validation [these validation steps and evidence types were used in the referenced tickets] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (49); Reports (209)]. 8) Update migration runbook and rollout gates (documented lesson): the recorded incidents updated KF‑MIG‑RB‑v1.4 and added a suffix‑handling requirement and explicit checks for service‑account exclusions, OU filter review, group membership parity and mobile client onboarding for subsequent waves [documented runbook updates after TI‑1700001 / TI‑1700004] [Data: Entities (916); Sources (63); Sources (50)]. Artifacts you must collect (the indexed records do not contain these raw artifacts) - AAD Connect sync run history and the Synchronization Service (miisclient) logs for the relevant date(s) - prior tickets reference running delta/full syncs but the corpus does not contain the raw AAD Connect log files, so get these from your AAD Connect server [records explicitly note the absence of raw engine logs in the indexed set; you must fetch them] [Data: Sources (54); Reports (209)]. - Azure AD sign‑in log entries showing CA evaluation (the records cite sign‑in logs were decisive evidence) - export entries for the sample users and for the service accounts around the failure timestamps [documented approach] [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50)]. - Task Scheduler Operational events (Event Viewer export / .evtx) from the internal servers showing the Task Scheduler error lines (the prior incident cites the message "Logon failure: the user has not been granted the requested logon type at this computer."). The indexed records show the message but do not embed the raw event file; ask server owners for the exported logs [Data: Sources (49)]. - Conditional Access policy export (policy JSON or screenshots) for the tenant baseline CA (the record describes KF70‑Require‑MFA and the exclusion applied, but does not include a policy JSON export). Export the CA policy to confirm Included and Excluded groups and save the export for audit [Data: Sources (50); Entities (754)]. Who should own each action (documented guidance) - Identity / AAD Connect engineering (BU Infrastructure - Identity) should own AAD Connect OU filter review, UPN reversion, and running delta/full syncs - prior incidents were owned by Employee 30 and similar identity engineers [documented] [Data: Sources (22); Sources (54); Entities (297)]. - Conditional Access / Security team should own the creation/population of the service‑account exclusion group and the CA policy edit (to exclude that group) - TI‑1700004 recorded these edits and owner duties [documented] [Data: Sources (50); Entities (755)]. - Endpoint / Mobile team should own device re‑enrollment and mobile guidance (re‑add as Microsoft Exchange or switch to Outlook Mobile) and Intune validations [documented] [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. When the indexed knowledge is insufficient - The indexed incident corpus documents the root causes and the remediation sequences that resolved the same four symptoms in prior cases (UPN writeback, OU/filter issues, CA applying to service accounts, EAS/Basic Auth mobile loops). However, the corpus does NOT contain the raw AAD Connect sync logs, the tenant CA policy JSON exports, nor the server Task Scheduler .evtx files needed to prove precisely which objects changed and at what timestamps in your environment. Those artifacts must be collected from your tenant and servers to complete the investigation end‑to‑end (and prior tickets explicitly requested and used them during diagnosis) [Data: Sources (54); Sources (50); Sources (49)]. Concise suggested immediate message to include in the handoff to Identity (documented handoff elements) - Short escalation note (use when you open the engineer ticket): "Second migration wave (week of ~06.01.25) produced: (1) cloud sign‑in OK but on‑prem apps failing (probable on‑prem UPN suffix overwrite); (2) cloud vs on‑prem group membership divergence (possible Migration_Phase2 OU / AAD Connect OU‑filter omission); (3) built‑in mobile mail clients looping (EAS/Basic Auth vs Modern Auth enforcement); (4) scheduled tasks failing for service accounts (service accounts may have been synced and are now evaluated by baseline CA). Please investigate AAD Connect OU filter, on‑prem UPN values, AAD Connect sync logs, Azure AD sign‑in logs (sample UPNs attached), and Task Scheduler event logs. Consider creating/using a dedicated exclusion group for non‑interactive service accounts if CA is blocking scheduled jobs (example used in prior case: KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA)." (This is a recommended handoff text built from the documented remediation actions and owner decisions in TI‑1700001 / TI‑1700002 / TI‑1700004) [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. If you want I can: - Produce a short checklist of the exact export fields to ask your AAD Connect admin and the server owners for (AAD Connect run logs, Sync Service report, Entra sign‑in log slice, Task Scheduler .evtx event export, CA policy export). (This is a recommended artifact to gather - prior incidents relied on precisely these exports but the exports themselves are not present in the indexed records.) - Or, if you prefer, I can draft the exact handoff ticket text/email to Employee 30 / BU Infrastructure - Identity including the ticket references (TI‑1700001, TI‑1700002, TI‑1700004) so the engineer has context from the historical cases cited above [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Sources (49)]. References (key indexed records I used) - TI‑1700001 - second‑wave UPN suffix overwrite, revert on‑prem UPNs, run full AAD Connect sync, validate on‑prem app access [Data: Sources (22)]. - TI‑1700002 - Migration_Phase2 OU / AAD Connect OU filter omission, update OU filter, run delta + full sync, reconcile group membership [Data: Sources (53)]. - TI‑1700004 - scheduled‑task outages: service accounts synchronized to Entra, CA requirement of interactive MFA blocked non‑interactive logons; remediation: correct AAD Connect scope and create CA exclusion group KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA; validate tasks [Data: Sources (49); Sources (50); Entities (755)]. - KF‑MIG‑RB‑v1.4 and migration runbook updates - per‑wave validation checklist items (cloud sign‑in, on‑prem app sign‑in, mobile mail, scheduled tasks/service accounts) and suffix‑handling requirement added after incidents [Data: Entities (916); Sources (63)]. - Mobile/EAS cases - built‑in iOS/Android Mail clients using EAS/Basic Auth blocked by Modern Auth enforcement; Outlook Mobile/OAuth worked; remediation via re‑add / Intune re‑enroll or app switch documented in KF70 mobile incidents [Data: Sources (4); Sources (5)]. Bottom line - The four different‑looking symptoms in your ticket map to a known single class of hybrid‑identity migration failures documented in the indexed records: (a) provisioning/template UPN writeback, (b) AAD Connect OU/sync scope errors causing group/object divergence, (c) legacy Basic‑Auth mobile clients blocked by Modern Auth enforcement, and (d) service accounts becoming subject to interactive MFA via CA after being synchronized. The prior incidents were resolved by the combination of: revert on‑prem UPNs + delta/full sync; fix OU filter + full sync + Graph membership reconciliation; create CA exclusion for non‑interactive service accounts and correct AAD Connect scope; and re‑onboard mobile clients to OAuth/Outlook Mobile. All of those fixes are recorded in the incidents cited above [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Sources (49); Sources (50); Sources (4); Sources (5)]. If you want me to draft the exact evidence request (file names, fields, and the ticket handoff text) to send to your Identity/AD Admins and server owners, tell me "draft exports request" and I will generate it (this is a recommended next action - the indexed records show those exports are what engineers used, but the records do not contain the exports themselves) [Data: Sources (54); Sources (49)].

question_text:
Hi IT, we need help asap because it feels like the whole company is falling apart since the weekend changes. I'm trying to describe it without guessing, but it's a mess.

1) In D365 (F&O) the Financial Reports / Report Designer thing is basically broken. It either loads forever or errors out. What's super strange: older reports suddenly show the author as 'unknown' and sometimes it says a report 'cannot be found' even when we used it last week. This happens in both test and prod, so we can't even use test as a workaround.

2) Sales: the desktop mail app is acting up. People can receive emails, but anything they send just sits in Outbox and later fails with a connection-style error. If they send the same email from the browser mailbox it goes through. Mobile seems fine too.

3) External partners: in Teams they are shown as Offline all day even while they are actively writing to us. Chats arrive late and sometimes calls to them do not ring. Internals are fine.

4) Zurich site: external calls in Teams connect, the other side says they hear us, but on our side it's dead silent (zero audio). We swapped headsets and speakers and it's the same. Internal calls are ok.

5) Basel site: external calls connect and then drop after about half a minute almost every time, like a timer. Internal calls are ok.

6) Also with the new marketing hotline numbers: calls to the old main number work, but calls to the new number range fail instantly. The provider claims they deliver the call to our edge.

7) Printing is chaos too, but it's not one single thing. A new employee in Basel sees literally no printers at all, even though everyone next to them can print. Another user can see the printer but gets 'Access denied' immediately when printing. And for the rest of us: jobs show up in the queue and then just sit there forever like nothing is processing.

8) One more: in Basel the main office printer sometimes shows jobs for a second and then they vanish, while other printers in the building still work.

People keep blaming laptops, docks, and Windows updates, but all of this started at basically the same time after the weekend work. Is this one connected change or just unlucky timing? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Below I separate (A) what the indexed incident records explicitly document (symptoms, root causes, diagnostic artifacts and the exact remedial steps that were actually performed in prior incidents) from (B) recommended next steps where the records do not provide the live artifacts needed to fully resolve your case. I end with a short, prioritized remediation/escallation checklist you can action now. A - What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence you can cite) - Overall classification precedent: multi‑service, multi‑site incidents that cross identity/mail/Direct‑Routing/print domains were handled as engineering (2nd‑level) incidents and required cross‑team escalation in the indexed cases [Data: Sources (0, 8, 16, 23, 28)]. 1) D365 Financial Reports / Report Designer - Prior incidents with the same symptom class required application‑team and vendor escalation, FRDB/Data‑Mart resets and Microsoft support case opening (example: FRDB/Data Mart remediation and Microsoft support case #2404050050000982). The recorded remediation included FRDB / Data Mart actions and escalation to Microsoft support [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)]. 2) Desktop Outlook clients: messages stuck in Outbox but OWA/Mobile OK - Prior cases showed Exchange Event Log evidence with "535 5.7.3 Authentication unsuccessful" and tenant Security Defaults / Conditional Access blocking SMTP AUTH for legacy clients; remediation recorded in the ticket was to selectively re‑enable SMTP AUTH or adjust Conditional Access and then monitor mailflow [Data: Sources (32)]. 3) Teams external presence / chats shown Offline - A documented incident (TI‑1607314) found tenant External Access / Presence settings (AllowPublicUsers, AllowFederatedUsers, EnablePresencePrivacyMode / Presence Sharing policy) had been tightened and that reverting/adjusting these settings (via Teams admin / PowerShell) restored external presence; Teams Admin Center / Get‑CsTenantFederationConfiguration were used during troubleshooting [Data: Sources (26)]. 4) Zurich - one‑way audio on external Teams calls - The Zurich one‑way audio case was traced to a weekend firewall security‑hardening change that limited outbound UDP ports (allowed only 3478-3481 and blocked the dynamic RTP range). Temporary opening of the wider RTP range restored media in tests; a permanent correction aligned the firewall to the Direct‑Routing / media‑flow blueprint [Data: Sources (23); Reports (5)]. 5) Basel - external calls drop after ~30 seconds - The Basel case was diagnosed as a NAT/UDP session timeout mismatch on the Voice VLAN (session timeout ~30s), validated with SBC/session tests, and fixed by aligning NAT/session timeouts to the Direct‑Routing blueprint; SBC logs showed SBC‑originated BYE at ~29-31s during the failing calls [Data: Sources (16); Reports (10)]. 6) New hotline / new number‑range failing instantly - The recorded investigation shows the carrier confirmed delivery of calls to the SIP trunk/SBC, but the SBC translation/normalization table lacked the new range. Applying a temporary translation rule validated call delivery; a permanent translation/routing update was then applied and validated [Data: Sources (8)].
